data.title: Sunrise Realty Trust: High-Yield mREIT Captures Growth In Florida And Texas
data.description:
As a real estate investor, I recently analyzed Sunrise Realty Trust (NASDAQ:SUNS), a new mortgage REIT focused on loans for commercial real estate in southern states. It was launched over a year ago by two investors with experience in real estate lending and local expertise in the South. They live in Florida. Based on my research, I rate SUNS a Buy. The most compelling reasons for this rating are a strong management team, migration to the south, and the impact of monetary policy.

Sunrise has a strong management team with previous experience managing publicly traded REITs. The Chairman actually founded a public REIT before SUNS, so he knows what is needed to get it up and running. The rest of the team has experience at reputable companies such as Fifth Street, Goldman Sachs, PwC, CIT Group, and Alliance Bernstein. Given their experience, the founders have existing relationships that help to create deal flow, which can accelerate the company's ability to scale up.

The southern states have unique demands for real estate that are on trend. There is a strong migration to the southern states, especially places such as Texas and Florida, that don't have any state income tax and are friendly to business. For example, Tesla just moved its headquarters from California to Austin, Texas to capitalize on tax breaks and the burdensome California regulations. Citadel moved from Chicago to Miami, Florida. These big moves create demand for several types of properties such as office, industrial, and residential space as the migration overwhelms current infrastructure.

As the Federal Reserve is in a rate-cutting cycle, lower rates tend to benefit mortgage REITS by lowering the cost of capital. These savings can be passed on to the lender, who can then afford to borrow more or is in a better position to repay the loan. Since the company just started lending, it is unlikely they would benefit from prepayment income, but it depends on how quickly (or not) the Fed lowers rates.

Corporate Governance: Strong & Accomplished

Leonard Tannenbaum, the founder, has an extensive list of related accomplishments, making him a credible leader for a mortgage REIT start-up. In his previous experience, Leonard founded Fifth Street Capital, an alternative credit asset manager, lending to developing middle-market businesses in multiple industries. Fifth Street ultimately managed $5 billion in assets in private investment vehicles and three public companies, including two BDCs. He eventually sold the company to Oaktree Capital Management in 2017. Tannenbaum holds a B.S. in economics and an MBA in Finance from the Wharton School. He is also a CFA.

The CEO is Brian Sedrish, who was previously the Head of Commercial Real Estate Acquisitions Special Situations for Deutsche Bank. SUNS's website adds he has "over 20 years of leadership in real estate private equity, focusing on institutional commercial real estate."

The Board is comprised of executives with vast real estate experience who have worked at commercial real estate organizations such as Cushman & Wakefield, further adding strength in advisory capabilities to the executive team.

Migration to the Southern States

The current migration trend to southern states will benefit companies such as SUNS, which is headquartered in Florida. According to the U.S. Census Bureau, between 2021 and 2022 the southern states had the largest growth in population. Florida was ranked #1 with +1.9% growth. Texas was #4 with +1.6% population growth. Pew Trust summed it up best:

Population growth in southern states outpaced all other regions in the second full year of the COVID-19 pandemic. Florida was the fastest-growing state for the first time since the 1950s, expanding by 1.91% between July 2021 and July 2022… population growth can lead to a rosier economic and fiscal landscape as a growing labor force stimulates new economic activity.

The map below illustrates the population change from the last time the Census was completed in 2022. The dark blue areas show the greatest migration such as Texas and Florida.

Current Portfolio: Small but Growing

As you can see from the current portfolio listed below, the two states with the first loans are Texas and Florida, mostly floating rate, with an astounding average of 15% yield to maturity. Management said they have over $1 billion in the pipeline, so I expect the company to ramp up very quickly from here. This is believable because the management team has built relationships in the area over the course of their careers, plus the demand for new construction real estate projects is growing with migration.

Valuation

I believe the stock is trading at a discount based on its limited history. On the Q2 Earnings call, SUNS management declared a $.42 "normal" dividend for the 4th Quarter. Assuming this was paid out on an ongoing basis, an annual dividend payout would amount to $1.68 per share. Using a basket of mortgage REITs that I follow, the average yield is 10.53%, which values SUNS at $15.95 per share. Assuming $.42 is the baseline dividend, SUNS can be expected to raise the dividend as they close more deals, driving the stock price higher. As the Federal Reserve is currently in a rate-cutting cycle, this adds demand for higher-yielding securities to replace the drop in yield of other securities.

SUNS has a market cap of $105.89 million (micro-cap), which is dwarfed by popular mortgage REITs such as the Blackstone Mortgage Trust (BXMT) at $3.25 billion, or MFA Financial (MFA) $1.3 billion, which I recently covered.

SUNS went public on July 9, 2024, steadily rising in value, as seen in the chart below.

Impact of Rate Cuts on Mortgage REITs

In the chart below, note how Chimera (CIM) starts to increase in value in anticipation of rate cuts, then peaks after the September 50 basis point cut.

MFA Financial experienced the same lift in value during the first rate cut:

Outlook

The next ex-dividend date is December 31st for a January 15th payout date. SUNS has already declared a $.42 cent dividend. I expect the Q3 Earnings call on November 7th to include an exponentially growing list of loan activities, which will add further confidence in the company and therefore be reflected in the stock price.

For the long-term investor, SUNS could also become an acquisition target. It takes a lot of energy to start up a mortgage REIT, creating value in an existing book of business that can easily be sold to another company or bolted on. For example, MFA Financial bought Lima One in 2017. There are advantages in scale through acquisition too, reducing costs by using existing infrastructure to handle the work. Since SUNS will be focused on the Southern states, a mortgage REIT company may want to add this geography as a diversifier, if they are concentrated in other regions. In real estate, it is always best to work with someone who has localized knowledge.

Risks To Rating

Mortgage REITs are sensitive to interest rates, therefore monetary policy over the long term will dramatically affect SUNS. Many economists are projecting two rate cuts for the remainder of 2024, totaling 50 basis points. If this does not materialize, SUNS will have higher borrowing costs to deal with, which affect the rate they offer the builder/project owner. Given the high demand for new real estate construction in the southern states, this is less of an issue as developers will be willing to pay a higher rate as they are chasing more profitable projects than areas with flat or declining demand.

As a new company, hiring and retaining the right people is essential to its success. The loss of a senior manager could alter relationships, important to bringing in new business. In commercial real estate projects, knowing whom to trust and who has a track record of success is critical in making a loan. New relationships expose the company to unknown risks such as incomplete records, or losing the deal to based on pre-existing relationships.

Natural disasters such as hurricanes in Florida are a risk to project success. The devastating effects of natural disasters could destroy work done on a new property, which could cause developers to go bankrupt, forcing foreclosure. Insurance companies in states such as Florida have become more apprehensive about covering exposures. In some cases, insurance companies choose not to cover states such as Florida altogether.

Bottom Line

In my opinion, income investors who are willing to take on the risks or mortgage REITS should consider SUNS as a buying opportunity. The most compelling reasons to invest include an experienced management team, migration to the southern states causing demand for new real estate projects, and the impact of rate cuts on mortgages. The risks of mortgage real estate investment trusts are primarily due to increases in interest rates, although we are currently in a rate-cutting environment. The company has the potential to increase its dividend, which would raise the stock value. Longer term, SUNS may be a company that is acquired by another mortgage REIT to benefit from the synergies of scale. The company reports Q3 Earnings on November 7th, at which time, I expect them to declare a dramatic increase in closed loan deals, raising expectations for 2025.

data.title: AMC Networks: Undervalued On All Accounts
data.description:
AMC Networks (Nasdaq: NASDAQ:AMCX) is a media and entertainment company that is currently trading at all-time lows. The company is facing a heavy debt-load and suffers from cord-cutting trends in their legacy business segment. Amid a turnaround to focus on high-growth segments like their streaming service and also maximize cash-flow, there is real pessimism in the market and possibly even fears of bankruptcy, with some of the bonds trading at over 15% yield.

Why is AMCX Down?

There are several factors as to why the stock is currently trading at extremely distressed levels. We will review some of the points that are, in our opinion, most important for AMCX's stock performance.

Cord Cutting

Cord Cutting refers to a trend of consumers cancelling their television subscriptions ("cable") and instead consume content through other services such as online streaming services like Netflix. Like other legacy media companies that benefit from cable television, AMC Networks suffered from declines in their cable business.

Non-streaming subscription revenues declined steadily and are continued to head lower in the years to come. With cable making up for a significant share of the company's total revenue, Cord Cutting contributes negatively to the stock.

Interest Expense

As many other media and entertainment companies, AMC Network has taken on large amounts of debts to finance content creation and growth in the years of zero-interest loans. A higher interest environment now puts pressure on the company's bottom line. Higher interest rates also make it harder for the company to refinance existing debt in the future, or take on further debt to fund new growth.

Media Companies

Partly due to the problems mentioned above, additionally to a downward trend in the ad market, media and entertainment companies with exposure to cable television as a whole have suffered in the last couple of years. While AMC Networks' stock performance has been especially bad, it does fit into the trend of comparable companies in the industry.

Cluster Risks

AMC Networks seemingly relies on a couple of big shows and franchises. We will review the company's portfolio content more in-depth further down below in our article. However, we want to emphasize that the concentration of their portfolio is a risk for the company, as dwindling interest for one of their top franchises could cause significant declines for the company's top-line. Namely, The Walking Dead and its spinoff shows are crucial for the company's success.

Dilution

While outstanding shares for the company has only slowly risen since 2022, there is a significant risk of dilution in the future. With the high debt load of the company and the high yields the bonds are trading yet, we could see a dilutive capital raise in the future. The convertible bonds outstanding further contribute to the negativity surrounding the stock and show the management's willingness to risk dilution of current shareholders.

As per the company's most recent 10-Q, at the end of Q2 2024, there were $143.8m of convertible bonds due February 2029 outstanding. The initial conversion price is around $12.74. A conversion at that price would mean current shareholders would be diluted by 25%, posing a significant risk and significant upwards resistance for the stock price.

Valuation

With the company being able to post an impressive TTM cash flow multiple of less than 1, it is clear that the price multiples do not tell the full story, as the company is highly leveraged. For our valuation, we will look at EV multiples instead.

With streaming revenue continuing to grow and make up a bigger part of the company's revenue, we believe that the discount the market applies is too harsh.

Streaming

With the company's streaming segment being the company's main bet on growth, it is an important factor in trying to value the company.

With streaming revenues only seeing modest growth, it will take a while before the company will be able to absorb declining revenues from their legacy segments with their streaming revenues. However, as long as the company stays cost-efficient while experiencing revenue declines in other segments, streaming will continue to increase their share in revenues in a healthy way.

Company Valuation

To calculate a fair Enterprise Value, we will look at some of the company's numbers based on the company's SEC filings and information found in their press releases.

Looking at the company's TTM Free Cash Flow, we see that the company was able to generate $404m in Free Cash Flow in the last 12 months of operations. If we look at comparable companies WBD (Nasdaq:WBD) and PARA (Nasdaq:PARA), we can estimate a fair EV/FCF for AMCX.

As per FactSet, WBD has a TTM FCF of $6.74b. With a current Enterprise Value of $57b, WBD is trading at an EV/FCF of 8.46. WBD being a much larger company than AMCX, with higher-quality balance sheet assets, so a significant discount being applied to AMCX would be justified.

PARA has a TTM FCF of $907m and an EV of $19.76b, trading at an EV/FCF 21.79. As reviewed on my last article on PARA itself is currently in the midst of a recovery, making a comparison much more difficult.

Considering similar companies and applying a significant discount to the multiple of WBD for example, we can assume a conservative EV/FCF multiple of just 5, landing at an estimated EV of $2b.

Looking at the company's TTM Adjusted Operating Income, we see that the company was able to generate $579m in Adjusted Operating Income in the last 12 months of operations. With our comparable companies WBD and PARA not reporting Adjusted Operating Income, we will estimate a fair multiple for AMCX based on the other companies' comparable metrics.

As per FactSet, WBD has a TTM EBITDA of $7.29b. With a current Enterprise Value of $57b, WBD is trading at an EV/EBITDA of 7.82. As discussed above, also on this metric, a considerable discount for AMCX is justified.

With a TTM EBITDA of $1.97b and an EV of $19.76b, PARA is currently trading at an EV/EBITDA of 10. Again, even when pointing at PARA's current struggles, a discount of AMCX compared to PARA is justified.

Considering similar companies' multiple and also applying a slightly lower multiple than for EV/FCF, we will assume a conservative multiple of just 4. With an EV/Adj. Operating Income multiple of 4, we land at an estimated EV for AMCX of $2.3b.

The company's 4.25% bond due Feb 2029 is currently trading at only 71 cents on the dollar. With trailing cash flows covering the company's net debt in just 4 years, we also believe the risk of bankruptcy of the company is highly overstated, making the bonds an interesting investment opportunity at high yields.

Taking the middle of our two estimations, one based on Adj. Oper. Income and one based on FCF, we land at an estimated EV of $2.15b. We want to note that based on our comparable companies, even our estimations are on the low end, making this a pessimistic fair estimate for AMCX.

With net debt of almost $1.6b, this means there is an estimated equity value of $550m. At a current market cap of less than $350m, this means a possible upside of more than 50%. After a theoretical price increase of 50% to $11.70, the price would still not reach the $12.74 bond conversion price, thus being safe from heavy dilution (for now).

Risks

With the company's dual class share structure and majority control being with the Dolan family, there is significant risk for other shareholders. Incentives and interests of majority shareholders with other shareholders are often not fully aligned, as can be seen by the recent Paramount fiasco.

As mentioned before, there is significant risk stemming from the company's debt load and risk of dilution. With the recent convertible bond issue being a sign of management's willingness to risk dilution, shareholders should be aware of this risk.

The dependence on AMC Network on a handful of franchises could become a significant risk for the company. In the fast paced world of filmed entertainment, trends can quickly shift. A new show can flop, even if the franchise itself was successful. A recent and prominent example of this being the Joker.

What to Look out for

With AMC Network's Q3 2024 report being expected in less than a month, we are eager to see the company's results for the quarter. Especially important is the rate of decline in the company's legacy business, the growth of their streaming operations and the free cash flow.

In general, we are closely monitoring if the company will be able to continue to show strong cash flow generation and the continued ability and willingness to pay back expensive debt.

data.title: XLV ETF: Time To Start A DCA In Healthcare Sector
data.description:
Everyone who reads my analysis knows very well my long-term portfolio perspective. It's pretty simple: some stock segments are destined to be profitable, and I want to allocate my income and cash flow into those segments whenever their prices become attractive. This week, in addition to starting a DCA (Dollar Cost Averaging) strategy in the agribusiness stock segment, I decided to begin a DCA plan in one of the strongest sectors in the world: healthcare. Although I usually prefer to focus my investments on the U.S. market, I’m going to explain why I made this choice. For that reason, I chose The Health Care Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLV).

Why XLV

XLV is the largest fund in the healthcare passive investment market, with an AUM of $41.12B. It has a very low expense ratio of 0.09% and a dividend yield of 1.48%. I typically perform a calculation that might make some of my fellow analysts nervous: the “net dividend yield,” which is the spread between the dividend yield and the expense ratio (excluding tax considerations). I do this to help in comparing ETF peers. In this case, the net dividend yield is 1.39%, meaning that, first and foremost, investing in this asset gives you an approximate positive return of 1.39% annually. The dividend yield has shown a steady growth, and for a long-term investor, that’s a good sign in my opinion—take a look at this.

XLV holds only 63 stocks, but in my opinion, this is not necessarily a critical issue. That’s because, in addition to being a thematic ETF, it is also focused on the U.S. stock segment, so it follows a geographical selection criterion. Perhaps a peer analysis can help resolve any doubts.

Peers analysis

What are the peers of XLV? This is the list shared by Seeking Alpha.

Now, I’ve customized it by excluding biotechnology ETFs and adding some interesting passive funds focused on the healthcare sector.

It’s easy to see that the expense ratios of the peers are higher, and their AUM is lower. The performance chart reveals an intriguing truth: the performance of the three most commonly used ETFs in the healthcare sector is very similar (And the “net dividend yield” of XLV continues to be higher than that of its peers).

However, the best performer was Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Health Care (RSPH).

The decision to distribute the holdings in an equal-weight format has given this fund a good alpha compared to other passive funds. This has been true since 2011, when the last bull market decade began.

In a sideways market and during bear markets, the equal-weighted approach underperforms compared to other passive funds. For example, let's take a look at the standard deviation: the standard deviation of RSPH is 16.29, higher than XLV’s, which is around 11. You can see this in the “risk section” on the profile page of Seeking Alpha.

So, it comes down to a personal choice. From a portfolio perspective, I believe it’s correct to maintain exposure based on a capitalization-weighted approach. This is because it’s more dynamic and makes the portfolio more adaptable to any “market changes.” Therefore, in this case, I’d like to confirm my intention to start (or continue) a DCA on XLV.

DCA Strategy

To achieve that, I will add a simple RSI to the performance chart, setting it on a daily timeframe, with a 14 to 21 period. I've mentioned this in many of my articles, but I want to emphasize it again: technical indicators are not very useful when trading, trust me. However, in the context of a DCA plan, I have to admit that they help me a lot in identifying good entry points to start accumulating something I’m interested in.

First, take a look at my investment choice: XLV.

In a bull market, like the one XLV has been in since 1800, there isn’t really a perfect time to enter. Essentially, you could choose to buy at any point, and the maximum drawdown would only impact your portfolio for around five years, similar to the S&P 500. How can we develop a strategy that provides good alpha to our portfolio? By implementing a “smart DCA.”

The idea is to start a DCA (with fairly large tranches) whenever the RSI falls below the neutral zone—ideally below 40 (though this depends on how you've set your indicator)—and stop once it rises above it again. By doing this, you will significantly reduce your average purchase price.

In blue, you can see the chart zones where you would have bought.

In certain bull market periods, a lot of time can pass before that strategy gives you an entry point. I think a good analyst should adapt the entry point accordingly. For example, after the big drop in 2020, it was possible to anticipate the entry point by focusing on the previous RSI low.

I think we are currently in a period very similar to that, so it could be a smart choice to adjust our entry points in the same way.

Why not choose a global ETF? Focusing entirely on the U.S. market is a risk!

The recent decrease in the XLV ETF is due to a contraction in Eli Lilly and Co. (LLY) and UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH). Regarding the first one, the drop is related to news involving the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), but I don’t believe this is a strong reason to negatively impact the company’s financial results. LLY has a diversified business that extends beyond this specific issue, for example, you can read more about that here. As for UNH, the decline is due to the latest earnings review, and you can find a good analysis here.

The risk is that XLV’s heavy concentration on U.S. companies, in this specific period, could affect the healthcare sector more than our DCA's technical expectations, especially since I am entering slightly earlier than my usual entry points.

Would a global alternative make a difference?

The global alternative to XLV is the iShares Global Healthcare ETF (IXJ), with 127 holdings and the following geographic distribution.

71% of the exposure is to the U.S. market, which means that even a global solution is heavily dependent on U.S. market dynamics. Additionally, we need to consider that the beta of non-U.S. healthcare stocks is close to 1, meaning that investing in a global alternative does not provide significant advantages. For example, IXJ has an expense ratio of 0.41%, a standard deviation of 12, and a lower total return (with European stocks also being more volatile)

Conclusion

I believe this could be a good time to consider continuing a “smart DCA” strategy in the healthcare sector. However, this may differ for you, depending on your strategy or risk tolerance. That said, I believe the data speaks for itself: XLV continues to be the best option on the market when it comes to passive funds focused on the healthcare sector.

data.title: TSMC Q3: The Ultimate AI Weather Vane
data.description:
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (NYSE:TSM) reported better-than-expected earnings for its third fiscal quarter on Thursday, lifting not only TSMC but the entire chip sector up. The strong earnings report, which showed quite significant earnings and free cash flow explosion at the chip manufacturer, came just days after Netherlands-based chip manufacturer ASML Holding (ASML) crashed on a decline in net bookings for its lithography machines. I have since explained why I bought the crash. I believe TSMC is and will remain a top bet for investors seeking exposure to the AI industry, and the company is facing a very attractive demand picture in the near future.

Previous Rating

I recommended Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing consistently throughout the last year as the chipmaker benefited from accelerating demand for AI chips. I also aggressively recommended TSMC ahead of the very strong earnings release: Why Taiwan Semiconductor Is A Strong Buy Ahead Of Q3 Earnings. TSMC's order books are filled, which is why the company submitted a strong revenue outlook for Q4 as well. I believe TSMC is set for long-term earnings growth and the high-performance computing segment is going to be a catalyst for a continual upside revaluation.

TSMC Beats Estimates

It was an earnings release that couldn't have been better if you invented it: TSMC beat expectations on both the top and bottom line, revenues surged 39% year-over-year in Q3'24 while earnings soared 54% year-over-year.

The earnings beat was significant as well: TSMC reported adjusted earnings per share of $1.94 which beat the consensus estimate by $0.15 per share. The top line came in at $23.63B, which also beat the average prediction by $338.4M.

TSMC benefited once again from accelerating demand from the high-performance computing sector, which was responsible for 51% of the company's revenues in the third fiscal quarter. HPC-related revenues surged 11% year-over-year due to strong demand for AI-optimized chips that help customers stem demand for artificial intelligence workloads.

TSMC generated NT$759.7B in revenue - which calculates to $23.5B - showing a year-over-year growth rate of 39%. Most importantly, TSMC's AI-driven growth is very profitable, both in terms of earnings and free cash flow. This is an important realization as many investors have been both worried about the durability of AI-related spending as well as the degree to which AI spending is profitable.

In the case of TSMC, the semiconductor company has been able to translate AI-driven spending tailwinds into real earnings growth. In the third quarter, TSMC reported NT$12.5 in earnings per share, which showed 54.2% earnings growth year-over-year. TSMC's free cash flows are looking even better, with the chip company reporting NT$184.9B, showing 171.8% growth.

Importantly, TSMC managed to deliver a significant gross margin beat in the third quarter as well: The chipmaker reported a gross margin of 57.8%, showing a significant 4.6 PP margin expansion compared to the previous quarter, Q2'24. The company also beat the top-end of its gross margin guidance by 2.3 PP, indicating strong pricing power for TSMC.

Outlook for Q4'24

TSMC guided for $26.1B to $26.9B in revenue for the fourth quarter, which implies a revenue growth rate of 35% year-over-year. Meaning, the company expects its revenue momentum to continue. In terms of gross margins, TSMC sees a range of 57-59% in Q4'24 which implies that the semiconductor maker sees gross margin expansion potential of up to 1.2 PP quarter-over-quarter.

TSMC's Valuation

The reason why I like Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing, even more so than ship design companies such as AMD (AMD) or Nvidia (NVDA), is that TSMC is strategically located at the very heart of the chip manufacturing industry and therefore is set to benefit from increasing AI-related chip spending. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing also owns more than 60% of the chip manufacturing market, making it all but impossible for companies to circumvent TSMC.

This market position creates a moat (a market entry barrier) and strong pricing power, which has been reflected in a growing gross margin trend. Still, shares of TSMC are not especially expensive, allowing room for valuation growth.

TSMC is currently valued at a forward P/E ratio of 23.8X, based off of FY 2025 earnings, which represents an 18% discount to the industry group average P/E ratio of 29X. The industry group includes Nvidia, AMD, and Broadcom (AVGO)... which are AI plays for investors as well that benefit from growing CapEx in the market for AI-optimized GPUs.

In my last work on the chipmaker, I argued that TSMC could have a fair value in the neighborhood of $218, based off of a fair value P/E ratio of 26.0X. TSMC is also expected to see 30%+ annual EPS growth going forward, in large part because companies are desperate for new AI chips. This setup should benefit TSMC, especially because I believe, as I said before, that AI spending seems to be secular, not cyclical. Companies are investing heavily in long-term AI-capable hardware, and Nvidia and AMD both are moving to 1-year product release cycles in order to capture more of this surging demand. Since TSMC is positioned right at the heart of the AI revolution, the company is also the ultimate AI weather vane that will show investors the general underlying business trends in the industry.

If TSMC revalues to the industry group average P/E ratio of 29X, shares could have a fair value of $250 per share, implying a 22% upside revaluation potential. Given that TSMC just presented a strong revenue and gross margin outlook, I believe this could be a realistic longer-term stock price target for the chipmaker.

Risks with TSMC

Waning demand for AI chips, chiefly in the Data Center market, would be a red flag and potentially indicate demand weakness. This demand weakness would likely imply a weaker gross margin profile as well as slowing earnings growth rates. What would therefore change my mind on the chipmaker is if TSMC were to see a weakening gross margin trajectory and a slowdown in its top line. In the long term, however, I believe TSMC's strong market position will serve the company very well, and I see a risk profile that is skewed to the upside.

Final Thoughts

The third fiscal quarter was a very solid quarter for Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing and a number of data points indicated a very healthy business: 1) TSMC continues to benefit from red-hot demand for AI chip manufacturing which is led to a 39% Y/Y surge in revenues, 2) TSMC translated this demand to 172% Y/Y free cash flow growth in Q3'24, and 3) The revenue outlook for the fourth quarter, which implies a sequential gross margin expansion, indicates that there is, in fact, no slowdown in demand for AI chips. This should calm investors' nerves after ASML's third-quarter earnings report indicated some cautiousness on the part of chip customers. Investors who seek a long-term play in the semiconductor market and who want to benefit from TSMC's exceptionally strong and dominant market position may want to consider an investment in Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing.

data.title: Bank of America: Reasonable Value On Upcoming NIM Expansion
data.description:
At the risk of opening by stating the obvious, Bank of America Corporation (NYSE:BAC) hasn't quite enjoyed the same pop from higher interest rates that some of its peers have, and yes, I have JPMorgan Chase's (JPM) recent purple patch firmly in mind as I typed that. Unsurprisingly given that, BAC stock has been very soft in this period - virtually flat with dividends and trailing close peer JPM by a whopping 66ppt since the Fed's first hike of the cycle back in 2022.

While it is understandable that some folks will chalk this up to management dexterity (or lack thereof in this case), these are two G-SIBs we are talking about here. I am not a banker or professional analyst, but it would strike me as unlikely that these kinds of institutions are in the business of making significant directional "bets" (lacking a better word) on interest rates. With that, the underlying drivers of BAC's income performance are probably fairly nuanced, and a full review likely needs the entire rate cycle to play out first.

Given the usual caveats about predicting the future, BAC does look better placed now to capture significant net interest margin ("NIM") upside, and that can drive a nice pop in its earnings as profitability normalizes closer to management's target. With these shares trading for around 11x 2025/26 earnings, I think BAC can scratch out 10%-plus long-term returns from here. I am bullish on the bank and attach a "Buy" rating.

NIM Expansion Now Coming Into Play

I haven't covered BAC on Seeking Alpha before, but I have read plenty of other contributors' pieces. As you would expect from a crowdsourced site, views range from bullish to bearish. One thing that has been something of a constant in recent times is BAC's management coming in for criticism in the comments section - particularly in relation to the bank's securities portfolio.

Like many things bank-related, BAC's bond portfolio is complicated and serves more than one purpose, including as a kind of hedge to manage the potential net interest income ("NII") volatility associated with a chunk of its funding base (e.g., retail current accounts). While this was perhaps a headwind for BAC in the first part of the rate cycle, this structural hedge income should be a nice tailwind for BAC going forward based on the current forward curve.

Viewing its balance sheet as a whole, BAC's overall earning-asset pass-through rate has, not surprisingly, lagged JPM's by a decent margin since 4Q21 (i.e., the quarter preceding the Fed's first interest rate hike).

At the same time, BAC has also been a bit weaker than JPM on funding. BAC's deposit base is solid - costing it just ~2.1% last quarter including non-interest-bearing balances - and its deposit pass-through rate has been roughly the same as JPM's at around 40%. However, including various forms of wholesale funding, BAC's overall cost of funding its earning assets has increased by around 60% of the increase in the Fed funds rate compared to ~55% for JPM.

That has basically driven the bank's lackluster NIM performance, with BAC only seeing a ~15% net yield pick-up since 4Q21 versus a meatier ~60% over at JPM.

Bank NIM can basically be broken down into separate margins on both earning assets and funding sources, plus an "everything else" bucket that, among other factors, includes the yield spread (colloquially referred to as borrowing short-term to lend longer-term). For BAC, that means that pass-through rates on its assets and liabilities, the composition of its balance sheet, and the shape of the yield curve are all things that will feed into NIM going forward.

It also means that the absolute level of interest rates probably isn't all that important, explaining how BAC's NIM can be much lower now than it was five/six years ago, when the effective Fed funds averaged ~2%:

The various moving parts mean that concrete NIM predictions are difficult to make, particularly over the long run. Nonetheless, BAC does look set to gain more from lower funding costs than it will give up on the asset side, driving some nice NIM expansion over time. Sell-side analysts appear to agree on the direction of travel, with NIM seen heading toward 2.1% over the next 1-2 years. Management has also mentioned in the recent past that a roughly 2.3% NIM is their long-term view of where BAC should be:

So I'd say right now in terms of the 1.93%, we feel like we are under earning. We feel like that number is going to go up over time. It'll go up as net interest income goes up. But additionally, I think the balance sheet is likely to stay kind of flattish here. So the numerator is going to grow, the denominator is going to stay pretty tight here. So we think we're under-earning there. We think through a cycle, we got to get back to a more normal number like 2.30-ish over time. That takes a while. It's a grind, Mike, quarter-after-quarter, so that's where we're headed.

Alastair Borthwick, Bank of America CFO, Q2 2024.

This may look aggressive when looking at BAC's recent NIM in isolation, but it would basically only bring it back into line with its pre-COVID average, as per the chart above.

BAC held just over $2.9 trillion in interest-earning assets on its balance sheet last quarter, so that degree of net yield pick-up would be material in terms of earnings. This will take time to play out, and BAC's NIM did slip a single basis point in Q3 to 1.92%, but I am looking for NIM to push on nicely from here.

Given all that, BAC does appear to be under-earning at the moment, as management stated above. Third quarter net income applicable to common shareholders was $6.4 billion ($0.81 per share), mapping to an adjusted return on tangible equity of ~12% (adjusted in that I have removed AOCI losses from its balance sheet; BAC's reported ROTCE was 12.8% in Q3).

Applying a 2.3% NIM to BAC's current balance sheet would essentially add an extra ~$2 billion to quarterly net income. Given roughly $215 billion in current tangible common equity (again I have excluded AOCI from this), ROTCE would see a nice bump up to around the mid-teens level, which is also roughly what management thinks BAC's longer-term earnings power is:

And in terms of return on allocated capital, right now we're right around that 14%. We want to be 15% or higher for our shareholder.

Alastair Borthwick, Bank of America CFO, Q2 2024.

BAC Stock Still Looks Decent Value

With the above firmly in mind, I do think this stock is decent value for investors with a longer-term outlook. BAC's current $42.60 share price works out to a 1.62x multiple of tangible book value (or ~1.5x excluding AOCI). That implies a ~12.7x P/E based on Q3 reported ROTCE of 12.8%, which would fall to ~11x based on a ~15% ROTCE. Consensus EPS for 2025/26 also points to a roughly 11x forward P/E.

That kind of valuation doesn't need BAC to conjure up anything particularly special in terms of long-term growth to justify. BAC being the circa $3.3 trillion asset giant that it is, long-term growth in line with U.S. nominal GDP probably isn't an outrageous assumption - and BAC's tangible asset base has roughly matched GDP growth over the past decade or so.

Certain bodies like the Congressional Budget Office think that long-term nominal GDP growth will be around 3.5% from here. Assuming BAC does earn a ~15% ROTCE through the cycle, it would only need to retain around ~25% of its net income to fund that kind of growth (i.e., 25% retained income multiplied by a 15% return roughly equals 3.5% growth). The remaining 75% of its earnings should essentially be available to distribute to shareholders via dividends and buybacks.

On a P/E of 11x (or a ~9% earnings yield to flip it on its head), that does give these shares a credible path to ~10%-plus annual returns, with that consisting of ~3.5% underlying net income growth plus around 7% from capital distributions (i.e., 75% multiplied by 9%).

Summing It Up

Bank of America has been fairly lackluster over the recent interest rate cycle, especially compared to its closest peer/comp JPMorgan. While management has copped its fair share of criticism in terms of the underlying drivers of that, there is probably more nuance to this than meets the eye. With BAC looking better placed now that interest rates are coming down, the bank can start pushing on toward management's long-term goal of earning a mid-teens ROTCE.

With that, shareholders don't need BAC to move mountains regarding growth to turn that into solid returns. While this will ultimately take some time to play out, I think it is just about enough to tip the stock into a "Buy" rating for long-term investors.

data.title: DFAR: A Reasonable Deal To Gain Indirect Exposure To US REITs
data.description:
Among the better REIT ETFs I've been researching this week is the Dimensional US Real Estate ETF (NYSEARCA:DFAR), a $1.3 billion fund that has nearly 50% comprised of the top REITs in North America across diverse categories. It is also one of the cheapest with an ER of 0.19%, which might be an important consideration for long-term investors. The ETF was incepted in February of 2022, and since then, it has not yielded any meaningful capital appreciation, and the distributions have been lumpy, with a current yield of 2.3% in the RRM period.

Investing in REITs vs REIT ETFs

REITs have always attracted income investors because they're mandated to pay out at least 90% of whatever taxable income is declared every tax year. That's the basic requirement of a REIT, among other ownership restrictions, and the way it's structured is that the trust owns the underlying real estate assets, but may choose to be managed either internally or externally. In general, internally managed REITs are more attractive because the managers can focus on just that one portfolio and can better align themselves with unitholder interests.

As an investor, you get to own pieces of prime property without directly investing in them. You get exposure to the distributions as well as capital appreciation of the assets over time. That's why REITs can be so compelling.

The Risks of REIT Investing: An Example

On the flip side, investing in a REIT can be risky, particularly when the REIT is non-diversified. Take the example of office REITs and what's been happening to them over the past few years. Ever since the pandemic hit, they've been struggling with declining FFO (funds from operation). Office REITs, which primarily invest in office-space properties, were somewhat protected by long-term leases, and they held on until around the end of 2021, which is when things started to unravel.

SL Green Realty Corp. (SLG) as a good example. This New York office REIT was punished swiftly and severely by investors, even though it managed to keep its FFO relatively stable and even growing until the third quarter of 2021. The stock, however, has been struggling to get back to its pre-pandemic level, and that range-bound movement can be seen clearly in the price return chart below.

Remember, this is despite SLG showing impressive capital appreciation over the past year, delivering a price return of 145% since October 2023.

The only thing that allowed SLG to maintain its positive investor sentiment, in my opinion, is that it paid a couple of hefty special dividends in 2020 and 2021.

Moreover, management moved from quarterly dividend payouts to a monthly schedule, which gave them more flexibility without necessarily having to pay more in dividends. Unfortunately, the regular dividend kept dropping, from 31 cents a month (after the initial change from 90+ cents per quarter) to 27 cents in 2022 and 25 cents (past ten months.)

Investors can't be blamed for thinking this is a great investment opportunity right now, especially when the stock is trading at a sub-10x forward P/FFO, but if you'd held through the two of the toughest years since the pandemic hit, you'd be heaving a sigh of relief, at best. At worst, you'll be dreading that these wild swings in FFO persist beyond Q3, results for which were reported on October 16 to a lukewarm reaction from investors.

The reason I'm detailing what happened with SLG is that it is exposed to greater risk because its investments are concentrated in office properties, and what's more, this isn't the only REIT subtype to be hit by the pandemic. Retail REITs also took a nasty hit after the virus outbreak because consumers were no longer shopping physically due to the lockdowns.

The point here is that a REIT ETF is vastly different from a single REIT holding because it offers protection in the form of diversification. That doesn't mean your investment is 100% safe, of course, but that diversity ensures that your risk is spread across multiple REIT subsectors. Each of these subsectors has a major Achilles heel, so the more heels you have on the ground, the stronger your portfolio can stand against practically any attack other than a large-scale recession.

In many ways, a REIT ETF is also better than a diversified REIT because the portfolio managers have to be proficient in multiple areas of REIT investing, which dilutes their focus. That's not the case here - each holding of the ETF has its own portfolio managers who are highly experienced in those specific areas. Wouldn't you want your assets to be managed by specialists rather than Jacks-of-all-REITs? I would.

Another positive about REIT ETFs over standalone REITs is that even though you're not getting direct exposure to the individual REITs' distributions, you don't have to worry about constantly rebalancing your REIT portfolio to get the best gains out of the most well-performing subsectors. The fund manager does that for you, and in the case of DFAR, at a very nominal 0.19%.

What I Like About DFAR, Specifically

Let's get into the specifics of DFAR and why I think it's a good investment in the REIT ETF space.

One of the metrics I like to look at for any ETF is AUM growth, and it's important to do that in the context of price appreciation over that same duration. The reason is simple: it's easy to see why a fund might be attractive when things are going well and capital appreciation is happening in lockstep with asset growth, but what about DFAR, which has an abysmal price return of under 3% since inception? That's where the AUM growth really stands out. Of course, we're not discounting the organic NAV growth of the underlying REITs, but this type of steady addition to AUM is certainly encouraging. It implies that investor confidence in this REIT is both positive and stable.

The second major reason I like this REIT is the allocation to various REIT subsectors, so let's dig into that next.

A Brief Discussion on the DFAR ETF's Top Holdings

The ETF's top holding, Prologis, Inc. (PLD) is an industrial REIT and comprises 8% of the fund's assets. At a market cap of $120 billion, it is the largest of its kind, as well as one of the largest REITs overall. What I like is its historical AFFO growth rates in the range of around 8% to +9% over the past three to five years. Although analysts expect a slowdown in FY 2024, the next three years' estimates are between 8.4% and 11.3%, so even at the low end that would be around the historical average. That's good because it shows stability with the potential for some growth. With an FFO to Revenue ratio of roughly 70%, every dollar added to the top translates to 70 cents more at the bottom line.

What I like even more about DFAR's holdings is the 11% allocation to telecom tower REITs and 10% allocation to retail REITs. The former is comprised of a 7.5% weighting to American Tower Corporation (AMT) and 3.5% to Crown Castle Inc. (CCI), which together represent about $160 billion in market capitalization in this attractive REIT subsector.

The exciting part is that growth is almost inevitable in this sector. As more endpoint devices are deployed, the volume of wireless data will keep growing in a steady manner. The trends represented by this set of graphs from a little over a year ago are still very much valid and in play.

As two of the largest REITs in the cell-tower domain, both AMT and CCI will benefit not only from data and device proliferation, but also macro factors such as population growth and new technologies being brought to market at a rapid pace. We need faster and more reliable communication more than ever, and these REITs are a key component of the infrastructure on which that will be built.

The other allocation that, I think, is prudent is data center REITs, as represented in this portfolio by Equinix, Inc. (EQIX) and Digital Realty Trust (DLR). The combined $140 billion market cap positions these REITs well to capture the 10% CAGR projected over the rest of the decade. I suspect that doesn't factor in future workloads from AI, autonomous and connected vehicles, and other catalysts.

DFAR has weighted telecom tower and data center REITs at a combined 21%, which should support strong growth rates for the underlying assets over the medium to long term. This is balanced out by retail, self-storage, and healthcare, as well as some experiential and gaming REITs that aren't part of the Top Ten.

The undercurrent to all these REITs is the falling interest rate environment. I've said in many of my recent articles that I don't expect the Fed to keep making aggressive cuts, and I still stand by that; regardless, they'll keep coming down as and when inflationary and other economic indicators dictate their need to.

As an aside, a Democratic win in November might tilt things in favor of rapid cuts, but the Fed is not likely to give in to political pressure, in my opinion. The reason? The negative repercussions of rapid rate cuts now would be far too great on the economy, which is still resilient at the macroeconomic level but showing signs of distress at the microeconomic level; by that, I mean the bulk of consumers who are still burdened by the high cost of everything they touch - from staples too discretionary to credit.

In that scenario, though I might be painting a bleak picture for consumers, holding a REIT ETF like DFAR does make sense.

It's performed better than most of its peers (as selected by Seeking Alpha's default peers tool), has the lowest ER and much better liquidity than the majority of them, the lowest turnover ratio (attesting to the selection of its holdings), and among the highest yields in that group.

As such, I'm comfortable rating DFAR a long-term Buy, but with the caveat that there may be other gems in the REIT ETF space that I haven't come across yet. The much older JPMorgan Realty Income ETF (JPRE) looks a tad better from a price return perspective, but I'd need to do a full-on comparison to justify that assumption. For now, I'm happy to recommend DFAR to investors looking for indirect exposure to real estate. After all, when we're done with the dollar and gold and silver lose their shine, all we have is land. And maybe water, but we're not that far into the future yet!

Thanks for reading my work.

data.title: Archer Aviation: Dilution Risk Is Rising
data.description:
Archer Aviation's (NYSE:ACHR) share price has been under pressure in recent months, which could become a problem as a result of the company's capital requirements. Stellantis (STLA) has been sharing a fair portion of this burden, although likely expects to benefit through the manufacturing relationship. Stellantis has its own issues, though, and it is not clear how committed it will be if Archer's future begins to look shaky. Outside of funding needs and the risk of dilution, Archer's business is progressing steadily. I tend to think that Joby's (JOBY) lead in the space is widening, though.

I previously suggested that inflated expectations were a risk for Archer shareholders. While the company's share price has been volatile, it is currently down by around 7% since then. From a fundamental perspective, nothing has really changed in recent months, but the company's modest market capitalization and significant financing needs are becoming more of an issue.

Archer Business Updates

Recent regulatory milestones include:

  • The FAA finalizing Midnight's airworthiness criteria, enabling Archer to progress the type certification process.
  • Part 135 Air Carrier & Operator certification in June, which enables Archer to operate commercially in the US, once Midnight is type certified.

Archer is also nearing completion of for-credit testing of its composite airframe structural materials and environmental testing of system hardware. The company also continues to complete for-credit audits of various hardware and software elements of its aircraft.

In addition, Archer's certification efforts are progressing internationally, including in India, Korea, and the UAE. In particular, Archer believes that there could be an opportunity to deploy its aircraft in the UAE ahead of the US.

Archer conducted 50 flights in the fourth quarter of 2023 and over 100 flights were logged in Q1 2024. Archer more recently stated that it is ahead of schedule with its test flights, with the company completing 402 test flights so far this year, up from 230 flights at the end of the second quarter. This is a result of the company ramping up operations ahead of for-credit flight testing with the FAA, with the goal of demonstrating a commercial operations-like flight cadence (>15 flights per day).

Archer completed a transition flight with Midnight in June, flying at over 100 miles per hour. This is the second full-scale aircraft that Archer has achieved transition flight with. Archer plans on starting piloted flight testing this year to gather the data necessary to support type inspection authorization, after which FAA pilots will fly Archer's aircraft and perform for-credit flight testing.

Manufacturing

Manufacturing activity is still fairly muted at the moment, which is probably the result of capital constraints and Archer's testing timeline. Archer is already building its fleet of six aircraft that will be used for credit testing with the FAA. This is happening at Archer's California facility ahead of the construction of a larger facility in Georgia. Archer's high-volume manufacturing facility is expected to be completed by the end of this year, with an initial production rate of roughly one aircraft per month.

Archer has reached an agreement in principle on key terms of its planned contract manufacturing relationship with Stellantis. Prior to investing $55 million in July (upon Archer achieving the transition flight milestone), Stellantis had invested close to $250 million in Archer. Stellantis also plans on financing up to $390 million in manufacturing labor and CapEx. This is expected to cover planned manufacturing labor costs through 2030.

In return, Archer will issue shares to Stellantis on a quarterly basis, based on the costs incurred. Phase 1 of the relationship is expected to support production of up to 650 aircraft annually. Archer will also issue Stellantis $30 million of performance warrants that will vest based on the achievement of performance milestones.

Partnerships

Archer's ability to expand its relationships and order book continues to be the most positive part of the business. Archer recently signed an agreement with Signature Aviation for air taxi services, including the installation of rapid charging systems at terminals. Signature Aviation has the world's largest network of private aviation terminals. This is a potentially important relationship given that terminals will be one of the bottlenecks to scaling up supply.

Archer's planned air mobility network in the San Francisco Bay Area was strengthened by a memorandum of understanding with Kilroy Realty. Kilroy Realty's Kilroy Oyster Point campus in south San Francisco will provide a vertiport that connects to Archer's planned locations in Napa, San Jose, Oakland, and Livermore, where the company has existing relationships, including with Signature Aviation and Atlantic Aviation.

Archer also has plans for a Los Angeles air taxi network, including vertiports in locations like Los Angeles International Airport, USC, Orange County, Santa Monica, Hollywood Burbank, Long Beach, and Van Nuys. This network will involve partners like Signature and Atlantic Aviation and United and Southwest Airlines.

On the aircraft sale front, Archer recently signed an agreement with KakaoMobility, a Mobility-as-a-Service platform in Korea. KakaoMobility will purchase up to 50 of Archer's Midnight aircraft, with the goal of commercializing eVTOL operations in South Korea as soon as 2026.

Future Flight Global also plans on purchasing 116 aircraft and made an initial deposit when it signed the MOU. This represents a significant expansion of the company's order book and is the most positive recent development. Future Flight Global is expected to make significant pre-delivery payments later in the year when the definitive aircraft purchase agreement is signed. This could be meaningful as Archer has suggested down payments in the 1-2% range and pre-delivery payments of up to 50%.

Archer has a contract with the US DoD in 2023 with a maximum value of up to $142 million. As part of this, Archer recently delivered its first Midnight aircraft to the USAF to evaluate as part of its AFWERX Agility Prime contract. The next step is for AFWERX to conduct government-directed testing of the aircraft for the USAF and validate operational and military-specific mission concepts.

Financial Analysis

Archer's indicative order book is currently sitting at around $6 billion, which goes a long way toward justifying the company's current share price. Consideration needs to be given to the likelihood of this order book being fulfilled and over what timeframe, though.

Archer is targeting 40-50% gross margins, assuming 250 units produced annually with a $5 million ASP. Archer believes at this level of revenue (~$1.25 billion) it will also be able to achieve operating profitability, dependent on how much it chooses to invest in R&D and sales and marketing. This probably means that Archer will incur losses for at least something like the next 4 years.

Archer had $360 million in cash at the end of the second quarter, and subsequently added to this with a total of $230 million in equity capital raises. The company's free cash outflow has been sitting at around $100 million a quarter, though, meaning that this does not provide a particularly large runway (~18 months). Archer has suggested that its core expense base is currently sitting at around $75-80 million per quarter, with one-time expenses like materials and non-recurring engineering adding to costs.

Given Archer's arrangement with Stellantis and likely losses over the next few years, I would not be surprised to see Archer's share count end up at around 1 billion, undermining much of the company's potential returns.

Conclusion

Archer continues to target commercialization of Midnight within the next 18 months. Given the company's current order book, this could create significant shareholder value, if costs can be kept under control. Dilution is a large risk, though, which is compounded by the uncertainty that the certification process creates.

Outside of this, Joby appears to be better positioned. Joby's aircraft offer a higher top speed (200 mph versus 150 mph) and longer range (150 miles versus 100 miles). Joby also recently conducted a 523-mile hydrogen-electric flight. This type of capability could eventually enable eVTOL flights between destinations like Baltimore and Boston, or San Francisco and San Diego. The flight was performed by converting one of Joby's battery electric aircraft so that it could operate using hydrogen.

Joby's autonomy efforts have been boosted by the acquisition of Inras and Xwing. While this is more of a long-term initiative, Xwing's technology was recently used in the USAF's 'Agile Flag' exercise, with Joby's team recording more than 3,900 miles of autonomous flight between 9 military bases and public airports across California and Nevada. Autonomy will likely be a core capability for eVTOL companies for several reasons. It will help to ease the pilot bottleneck and lower prices to a point where demand is significantly higher.

Joby is also ahead on certification and manufacturing and has a much stronger balance sheet. As a result, the share price performance of the two companies has begun to diverge in recent months.

data.title: Morgan Stanley Vs. Goldman Sachs: Better Fixed Income Investment?
data.description:
In our previous analysis, we analyzed the stocks of The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (NYSE:GS), and Morgan Stanley (NYSE:MS) and concluded that Goldman Sachs outperforms Morgan Stanley in the investment banking segment, driven by its higher investment banking fees, brokerage revenue growth, and larger market share. In the asset management segment, Goldman Sachs surpasses Morgan Stanley with higher revenue growth and larger assets under management. However, when comparing financial ratios, Morgan Stanley demonstrates superior capital ratios and profitability. Taking all factors into account, despite Morgan Stanley's stronger financial metrics, we believed Goldman Sachs to be the superior company overall due to its dominance in investment banking, asset management and valuation.

In this analysis, we compare these companies’ fixed income securities to determine which company is better for fixed income investors. Both of these companies are highly similar, with their business models mainly encompassing investment banking and asset management, and are the top companies in these markets by market share. Firstly, we compare the two companies in terms of their fundamentals, based on the company’s segment performance and profitability focusing on their stability. Moreover, we compare the companies to determine which company has a stronger financial position based on key financial ratios. Finally, we compare the companies in terms of their fixed income securities ratings and coupon rates.

Fundamental Comparison

In the first section, we compare Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs to determine which company has better fundamentals for fixed income investors. Both companies are highly similar, with their businesses mainly focusing on investment banking and asset management.

As highlighted in our previous analysis, both companies have a strong market position, MS and GS are among the top companies by market share in IB and asset management and are well established in these markets. GS has experienced a concerning upward trend in its NPL ratio, increasing from 0.38% in 2019 to 1.57% in 2023. This rising trend signals deteriorating asset quality over the period, which is unfavorable. In contrast, MS has maintained a much more stable NPL ratio, starting at 0.49% in 2019 and gradually decreasing to 0.31% by 2023, demonstrating consistent asset quality management. This stability in MS’s NPL ratio indicates a stronger and more controlled approach to managing credit risk compared to GS's increasing NPL ratio, which reflects worsening asset quality. Moreover, GS has a higher NPL ratio compared to MS because GS has a higher NPL relative to its loans compared to MS. During 2023, GS had total loans of $160,939 mln and NPLs of $2,533 mln, whereas MS had total loans of 206,663 mln and NPLs of $635 mln. Thus, MS had 28% more loans than GS but had 75% fewer NPLs than GS.

Furthermore, in terms of the Provision Coverage Ratio, GS’s ratio has been trending downwards, with a peak ratio of 2.08 in 2019 and the lowest at 0.95 in 2021. Morgan Stanley’s Provision Coverage Ratio started lower at 0.39 in 2019 and gradually increased to 1.14 in 2023. The consistently lower NPL percentage coupled with a gradually increasing provision ratio reflects Morgan Stanley’s more conservative and effective risk management strategy compared to Goldman Sachs.

Overall, in terms of capital adequacy, asset quality, and liquidity, we believe MS outperforms GS. MS boasts a stronger capital adequacy ratio, with an average Tier 1 CAR Ratio of 17.7% compared to GS's 16.0%, indicating better capital resilience. MS also maintains a consistently higher Total CAR Ratio, benefiting from lower Risk-Weighted Assets. In terms of asset quality, MS has a significantly lower percentage of Non-Performing Loans ('NPL') at 0.42%, while GS's NPLs have been on an upward trend, reaching 1.57% in 2023, signaling deteriorating asset quality. Additionally, MS has demonstrated a more effective risk management strategy with a stable NPL ratio and a gradually increasing Provision Coverage Ratio, contrasting with GS's declining trend in this metric. Although GS slightly edges out MS in liquidity, particularly in the Quick and Cash Ratios, MS's overall liquidity position remains strong, with a better Current Ratio and a higher Loan-to-Deposit Ratio. While GS has shown a declining trend in liquidity over recent years, MS has maintained a relatively stable liquidity profile. Thus, MS's stronger capital adequacy, superior asset quality, and stable liquidity position make it the more resilient and stable option compared to GS.

On average, the total coupon rate across all companies is 6.538%, with JPMorgan having the highest coupon rate at 7.674%, and UBS Group having the lowest at 4.979%. Credit ratings indicate the creditworthiness of these institutions, and a lower average credit rating score indicates better credit standing. Barclays PLC has the worst average credit rating score of 7.3, while JPMorgan and Bank of America have the best scores at 5.3. The coupon rate to credit rating score ratio measures the yield a company offers relative to its credit risk. In terms of this metric, JPMorgan stands out with the highest ratio of 1.44%, suggesting a better return relative to its credit risk.

Comparing GS and MS, in terms of the coupon rate to credit rating score ratio, which reflects compensation relative to risk, Morgan Stanley has a slightly better ratio at 1.109% compared to Goldman Sach’s 1.09%. This is mainly due to Morgan Stanley having a better credit rating score compared to Goldman Sachs. Moreover, based on the average bond seniority ranking, MS has the 2nd highest seniority bond ranking, whereas GS is ranked 6th, highlighting that MS has more senior bonds compared to GS. Additionally, having more senior bonds is better for fixed-income investors, as more senior bonds would mean investors will have “first claims on the company’s cash flows” and have first claim on “company’s property, plant, or equipment” in the event of bankruptcy or liquidation. Overall, despite the lower coupon rate to credit rating score ratio, Morgan Stanley holds a better average credit rating score of 5.7, compared to Goldman Sachs' score of 6.7, indicating that Morgan Stanley is viewed as less risky. According to Fitch, some of its key rating drivers mentioned include its robust capital adequacy ratios like the Tier 1 capital ratio and that “MS is well-placed to comply with the recently proposed Basel III Endgame rules”. Additionally, in terms of Basel 3 endgame impacting CET1, the Federal Reserve announced plans in September to increase “CET1 capital requirements for the G-SIBs by 9%”, which would increase capital requirements for GS and MS, as these banks are a part of (G-SIBs). As a result, banks “will be required to hold more capital against assets, which will reduce the size of their balance sheets”. Moreover, PWC highlighted that in terms of Basel 3 endgame capital implementation, it will “take effect on July 1, 2025 and have a three-year phase in of capital ratio impact until June 30, 2028”.

Additionally, another factor highlighted by Fitch is the consistency of its profitability as “operating profit as a share of RWAs was 2.6% in 1Q24 and 2.7% on average for the 10-year period from 2013-2022, comparing favorably to both U.S. and international peers”. In the first section, we highlighted the superior profitability of MS vs GS in terms of ROA, ROE and net interest margin which are higher than GS.

Moreover, Fitch had also highlighted that MS had a “diversified business model”, particularly around Wealth Management and Investment Management completed by its past acquisitions such as Eaton Vance and E*TRADE. Comparatively, Fitch noted diversification weakness in GS's business model, where they highlighted that “GS's ratings have been constrained by its business model's weighting toward investment banking and trading activities”.

In 2023, AM accounted for 44.2% of MS's revenue, whereas GS's revenue remains heavily weighted toward IB, contributing 76.1% in the same year. In contrast, GS's focus on investment banking and trading activities indicates less diversified and more volatile revenue streams as highlighted in our first section where we analyzed the higher volatility as measured by the standard deviation of investment banking revenues compared to asset management.
